modules.module: M1: Why Most Side Projects Die and How Agile Fixes That

Claude.notes: Narrative architecture is exceptionally tight: each opening scene (Marcus, Priya, Jordan, Amara) creates exactly the problem the concept section answers, and the protagonists' specific failures map cleanly to the LIRA framework, sprint mechanics, and feedback-loop sequence. Voice is consistent across lessons — direct, peer-level, honest about the loop most twenty-somethings actually fall into. Weakest aspect is c2: the lesson tends to tell the learner the insight directly ("the kill pattern is...", "the right way to think about AI build tools is...") rather than letting Marcus's or Jordan's experience surface the conclusion through earned discovery. Cognitive framing is excellent — the lesson questions ("what's the actual reason your last project didn't ship?") force the learner into the diagnostic stance the module is teaching. Tab strip, lesson hero, key terms, callouts, and the four-page lesson rhythm (story → concept → quiz → lab) give learners clear orientation throughout. Labs are well-scaffolded with directive system prompts and offline fallbacks; quiz questions skew toward judgment over recall.
Claude.topIssue: Insights are often delivered expositionally rather than through the protagonist's lived realization — Marcus's story sets up the kill pattern beautifully, but the framework is then handed to the reader instead of letting the reader extract it. Tightening this would lift D1c2 from 3 to 5.

Gemini.notes: Technical content is broadly accurate but takes some informal liberties. The Agile Manifesto attribution (February 2001, seventeen developers, Utah ski lodge) is correct in date and headcount; the precise venue was Snowbird ski resort, which is fine to gloss. Definitions of Sprint, Backlog, User Story, Acceptance Criteria, Definition of Done, and Sprint Goal are standard and correct. The Velocity definition is reframed for solo builders ("honest self-knowledge") rather than the formal Scrum metric (rolling average of completed story points per sprint) — a defensible adaptation but worth flagging that the canonical definition is omitted entirely. The implementation-intentions claim references "research on habit formation" without attributing Gollwitzer (1999), the foundational source. Real-world cases hold up: Napster (1999, Shawn Fanning) is correct in spirit; Cursor, Replit Agent, Lovable, Bolt.new, and Claude are real and current AI build tools; The Mom Test (Rob Fitzpatrick, 2013) and Lean Startup (Eric Ries, 2011) are correctly attributed. Misconception prevention is the strongest part of D2 — the module actively counters "AI build speed = product progress," "task completion = shipping," and "friend feedback = demand signal." Quiz items reinforce these correct framings rather than undermining them.
Gemini.topIssue: Velocity is redefined for solo use without naming the formal Scrum definition (story points completed per sprint averaged over recent sprints), and implementation intentions are cited without crediting Gollwitzer's 1999 research. Both are easy attribution fixes that would harden D2c1.

Perplexity.notes: Currency is a clear strength — the AI build-tool landscape cited (Cursor, Lovable, Bolt.new, Replit Agent, Claude) accurately reflects what 17–25-year-old solo builders are actually using in late 2025 / early 2026. The fictional protagonist timestamps (Marcus March 2024, Priya October 2024, Jordan February 2025, Amara January 2025) are within the 12-month relevance window and don't feel dated. The lesson's central thesis — that AI tools amplify whichever direction you're already pointed — is a take that has held up through the past year of "vibe coding" discourse and is more relevant now than when it was first written. One currency concern: the lab JavaScript hardcodes the model ID `claude-opus-4-5` in the proxy call, which is two generations behind the current Claude Opus 4.7 (4.5 → 4.6 → 4.7); learners hitting this in 2026 will be running on a stale model unless the proxy silently upgrades. Lab executability is high — chat-based labs with directive system prompts and an offline fallback mean a learner can complete every lab without a working API key. Quiz questions emphasize judgment ("what's the most accurate assessment?", "what should you do this week?") over recall, which holds up across model generations. Capability delta is concrete: by the end of M1 a learner can sequence a backlog with LIRA, write a one-week sprint plan with calendar blocks and a definition of done, run an Observe → Interpret → Hypothesize → Test cycle, and apply the three-sprint pivot test.
Perplexity.topIssue: The lab proxy is wired to `claude-opus-4-5`, which is two model generations behind the current Claude Opus 4.7. Bumping to `claude-opus-4-7` (or letting the proxy default to the latest) would future-proof the labs and eliminate the only meaningfully dated artifact in an otherwise current module.
